questions.prompt: Antenna gain G is related to directivity D by:
choices: B. G = η_r × D (radiation efficiency × directivity)
questions.explanation: G = η_r × D. Directivity accounts for radiation pattern shape; efficiency accounts for conductor/dielectric losses. G ≤ D.

questions.prompt: Directivity D of an antenna is defined as:
choices: A. Ratio of maximum radiation intensity to average radiation intensity
C. Ratio of gain to frequency
questions.explanation: D = U_max / U_avg = 4π U_max / P_rad. An isotropic radiator has D = 1 (0 dBi).

questions.prompt: The Friis Transmission Equation gives the received power P_r as:
questions.explanation: Friis: P_r = P_t G_t G_r (λ/4πR)². The term (λ/4πR)² is the free-space path loss factor.

questions.prompt: The effective aperture A_e of an antenna is related to gain G by:
questions.explanation: A_e = Gλ²/(4π). For an isotropic antenna: A_e = λ²/(4π).

questions.prompt: An isotropic radiator has directivity of:
questions.explanation: Isotropic antenna: D = 1 = 0 dBi. A half-wave dipole has D ≈ 1.64 = 2.15 dBi.

questions.prompt: The half-power beamwidth (HPBW) of an antenna radiation pattern is the angle between the two directions where the power is:
choices: B. Half of (−3 dB) the maximum
questions.explanation: HPBW: angular width of the main beam at the −3 dB (half-power) points. Narrower HPBW → higher directivity.

questions.prompt: The radiation resistance R_rad of an antenna represents:
choices: A. Ohmic losses in the conductor
B. An equivalent resistance that dissipates the same power as the antenna radiates
C. The input impedance reactive part
questions.explanation: R_rad: equivalent resistance where P = ½|I|² R_rad gives the radiated power. For a half-wave dipole, R_rad ≈ 73 Ω.

questions.prompt: The input impedance of a half-wave dipole (in free space) is approximately:
questions.explanation: Half-wave dipole: Z_in ≈ 73 + j42.5 Ω. Shortening slightly makes it resonant (~73 Ω, reactive part = 0).

questions.prompt: Free-space path loss (FSPL) at distance R for wavelength λ is:
questions.explanation: FSPL = (4πR/λ)². In dB: FSPL_dB = 20 log(4πR/λ) = 20 log R + 20 log f + 147.55 (SI units).

questions.prompt: Antenna reciprocity means:
choices: A. An antenna radiates equally in all directions
B. Transmit and receive patterns and impedances are identical for a passive antenna
C. Antennas only work at one frequency
questions.explanation: Reciprocity: a passive antenna has the same radiation pattern, gain, and impedance whether transmitting or receiving.

questions.prompt: A parasitic element placed behind the driven element in a Yagi-Uda antenna, shorter than the driven element, acts as a:
questions.explanation: Reflector: parasitic element slightly longer than λ/2, placed λ/4 behind. Directors: shorter elements in front, increase gain.

questions.prompt: The polarization of an antenna refers to:
choices: B. The orientation of the electric field vector in the far-field radiation
C. The physical tilt angle of the antenna
questions.explanation: Polarization: orientation of E-field in the radiated wave (linear, circular, elliptical). Polarization mismatch reduces received power.

questions.prompt: The far-field (Fraunhofer) region of an antenna begins at distance:
questions.explanation: Far-field starts at R > 2D²/λ (Fraunhofer criterion). Near-field (reactive): R < 0.62√(D³/λ).

questions.prompt: A patch (microstrip) antenna is preferred for mobile applications because:
choices: B. Low profile, lightweight, and conformable to surfaces
questions.explanation: Microstrip patch: low profile, lightweight, easily integrated on PCBs. Trade-off: narrow bandwidth (~2-5%), moderate gain.

questions.prompt: The front-to-back ratio (FBR) of an antenna measures:
choices: B. Ratio of main lobe gain to back lobe gain
C. Input to output power ratio
D. Bandwidth to center frequency ratio
questions.explanation: FBR = G(0°)/G(180°) in dB. Higher FBR means better rejection of signals from behind the antenna.

questions.prompt: Which antenna types are primarily resonant (narrowband) antennas? (Select ALL that apply.)
questions.explanation: Dipole, monopole, and Yagi are resonant/narrowband. Log-periodic is a broadband frequency-independent design.

questions.prompt: A 10 GHz link has P_t = 1 W, G_t = G_r = 20 dBi (=100), and R = 1 km. The received power P_r in dBm is approximately: (λ = c/f = 0.03 m)
The effect of ground plane on a monopole antenna (λ/4 over perfect ground) is that it:
choices: B. Acts as a mirror, making it equivalent to a half-wave dipole with double the gain
C. Reduces radiation resistance to zero
questions.explanation: λ/4 monopole over perfect ground: image theory → equivalent to λ/2 dipole. R_rad ≈ 36.5 Ω (half of dipole's 73 Ω), gain = 5.15 dBi (dipole radiates in both hemispheres, monopole only upper).

questions.prompt: The aperture efficiency η_a of an aperture antenna (horn, dish) relates physical aperture A_p to effective aperture A_e by:
questions.explanation: A_e = η_a × A_p. For a parabolic dish, η_a ≈ 0.5–0.7. Gain = 4π A_e/λ² = 4π η_a A_p/λ².

questions.prompt: A circularly polarized (CP) antenna, compared to a linearly polarized antenna, is advantageous because:
choices: A. CP has twice the bandwidth
B. CP is immune to polarization rotation from Faraday effect or multipath
questions.explanation: CP antennas are insensitive to the orientation angle of the receiving antenna (Faraday rotation, satellite links, multipath).

questions.prompt: Antenna bandwidth is typically defined as the frequency range over which:
choices: A. Gain is greater than 0 dBi
B. VSWR ≤ 2:1 (return loss ≥ 9.5 dB) or gain drops less than 3 dB
questions.explanation: Common bandwidth definition: VSWR ≤ 2 (|Γ| ≤ 1/3, RL ≥ 9.5 dB) or within 3 dB of peak gain. Both criteria are used in practice.

questions.prompt: The gain of a parabolic dish antenna with aperture diameter D at wavelength λ is approximately:
questions.explanation: Parabolic dish: G = η(πD/λ)² where η ≈ 0.55–0.65. Large aperture → high gain (used in satellite dishes, radio telescopes).

questions.prompt: An antenna array achieves higher gain than a single element by:
choices: A. Using multiple elements with controlled phase/amplitude for constructive interference in desired direction
questions.explanation: Phased array: controlled phase between elements creates constructive interference (beam forming) in desired direction, increasing gain.

questions.prompt: A return loss of 20 dB corresponds to an approximate VSWR of:
questions.explanation: Return loss RL = -20log10|Gamma|. RL=20 dB gives |Gamma|=0.1, and VSWR=(1+|Gamma|)/(1-|Gamma|)=1.1/0.9=1.22.

questions.prompt: For fixed physical aperture and efficiency, increasing frequency generally causes antenna gain to:
choices: D. Alternate between high and low
questions.explanation: Gain of an aperture antenna is G = 4pi*eta*A/lambda^2. As frequency increases, lambda decreases, so gain increases for fixed A and eta.
